metadata.title: Positioning & Pillars: What Sets You Apart
metadata.playlist: Name It & Brand It — Your Strategic Identity
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 6 brief — Brand Strategy + Naming

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 2 of 7. Part 1B of the assignment has two outputs: a set of brand pillars, and a positioning matrix. The pillars are the handful of things you want someone to remember about you or your tool after a five-minute conversation. The matrix shows where you sit relative to three to five competitors or peers, and — critically — where the white space is. Both outputs answer the same question: what is different about this, and is the difference worth something? The `brandy` skill in the toolkit was built for this episode. Let's go.

props.segment: Positioning & Pillars: What Sets You Apart
props.command: Build 5 brand pillars + a competitive positioning matrix with named white space.
props.runningText: brandy skill handles the competitive audit → matrix; your job is naming the white space…
output: 5 brand pillars: Promise · Personality · Story · Differentiation · Experience
White space: the specific position nobody else holds

beats.narration_text: The five brand pillars. Each pillar is a named dimension of how you or your tool shows up. The first pillar is Promise — what people can reliably expect every time they work with you or run your tool. Not aspirational marketing language. A specific expectation: 'Every output includes a source citation and a confidence flag. If the confidence is below 70 percent, the conductor is notified before the output reaches the user.' The second pillar is Personality — three to five adjectives or short phrases that describe tone and style. Precise. Transparent. Direct. These are not aspirational either — they should describe how the work already feels. The third pillar is Story — your background, the audience need that motivated the tool, and the connection between the two. One to two sentences. The fourth pillar is Differentiation — the specific thing that sets you apart from peers or competitors in your field. This is the distilled version of the positioning matrix — one sentence that names what you do that they do not. The fifth pillar is Experience — how people feel after working with you or using your tool. Not 'impressed' — concrete: 'the analyst who runs this tool leaves with a ranked digest, a source list, and a documented audit trail, in under ten minutes.'

artifactLines: Promise: what people can reliably expect — specific, not aspirational.
Personality: 3–5 adjectives/phrases — how it already feels, not how you want it to feel.
Story: background + audience need + connection. 1–2 sentences.
Differentiation: the one thing you do that peers/competitors don't. One sentence.
Experience: how people feel after — concrete, not 'impressed.'
props.sparkLine: Pillars describe what's already true. Aspirational copy goes in marketing, not here.

beats.narration_text: The competitive positioning matrix. Pick three to five professionals or tools that work in the same space as you or your Madison tool. If it is a personal brand for a data science role, the comparators are other data science students or recent graduates at the same stage. If it is a startup brand for the tool, the comparators are tools that solve the same problem — news digest generators, AI content summarizers, data pipeline tools. For each comparator, document their strengths and primary approach. What do they do well? What is their main method or differentiation? Build a matrix — rows are comparators, columns are dimensions that matter in your field. For data tools: accuracy, transparency, speed, human oversight, output format. For personal brands: technical depth, domain expertise, communication, portfolio quality, human-in-the-loop design. After the matrix is built, look for the combination of columns where no competitor or peer scores strongly. That gap is your potential white space. One sentence naming it: 'No other tool in the matrix combines source-auditable AI output with a human-conductor gate before delivery.' That is the positioning. The brandy skill can build this matrix if you feed it the comparator list and the dimensions.

artifactLines: Rows: 3–5 peers or tools. Columns: 4–6 dimensions that matter in the field.
Per cell: strength level + primary approach. Use consistent scoring (high/medium/low).
Look for column combinations where no comparator scores strongly — that is white space.
White space sentence: 'No other [tool/person] in this space [does X + Y together].'
brandy skill: feed comparator list + dimensions → matrix + white space memo.
props.sparkLine: The gap nobody owns is your positioning. Name it in one sentence.

beats.narration_text: Two traps that cost Part 1B points. Trap one: pillars that fit anyone. If your Promise pillar says 'high-quality work delivered reliably,' you have described every professional. That is not a pillar — it is a default assumption. A pillar must be specific enough that a competitor reading it would say 'that is not what I do.' If you wrote 'transparent' as a Personality trait, the next sentence must say what transparency means in practice: 'every model output shows the source document and the confidence score.' Vague adjectives without concrete anchors are not pillars. Trap two: superficial competitive analysis. A positioning matrix where every competitor is described as 'good at AI but lacking in UX' and you are described as 'strong in all areas' is not analysis — it is self-promotion. Real competitive analysis includes at least one area where a comparator is stronger than you, and explains how your positioning accounts for that. If every cell in the matrix reflects favorably on you, the analysis is not honest. The grader is looking for someone who understands the landscape, not someone who surveyed the landscape and awarded themselves first place.

props.artifactHeading: Pillars that fit anyone · Superficial matrix
artifactLines: Trap 1 — Generic pillars: 'high-quality, reliable work' = every professional. Not a pillar.
Fix: each pillar must be specific enough that a competitor says 'that's not what I do.'
'Transparent' needs an anchor: 'every output shows source + confidence score.'
Trap 2 — Self-promotion matrix: if every cell favors you, the analysis is not honest.
Fix: name at least one area where a comparator is stronger, and explain your positioning around it.
props.sparkLine: Specific enough to exclude someone else. Honest enough to include your gaps.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Positioning and Pillars. Five brand pillars: Promise, Personality, Story, Differentiation, and Experience. Each specific enough that a competitor reading it would say 'that is not what I do' — vague adjectives without concrete anchors are not pillars. A competitive matrix with three to five named comparators and four to six dimensions; at least one cell where a comparator is stronger than you; one sentence naming the white space. The brandy skill handles the matrix if you provide the comparator list and dimensions. Superficial analysis costs Part 1B points. The matrix that reflects favorably on you in every cell is not analysis. Episode 3 is naming — ten names, five descriptive and five creative.

props.artifactTitle: Positioning & Pillars: What Sets You Apart
artifactLines: 5 pillars: Promise · Personality · Story · Differentiation · Experience.
Each specific enough to exclude competitors. Vague adjectives need concrete anchors.
Matrix: 3–5 named comparators · 4–6 dimensions · honest cells (at least one competitor wins).
White space: one sentence naming the combination nobody else holds.
brandy skill: feed comparator list + dimensions → matrix + white space memo.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Two prompts — one for the pillars, one for the matrix. First, for the pillars, paste this: I need to write five brand pillars for [personal brand / my Madison tool]. Here is my UVP from Episode 1: [paste it]. Here is my Differentiation statement from the matrix: [you will fill this after the matrix]. Draft all five pillars — Promise, Personality, Story, Differentiation, and Experience. For each: write one to two specific sentences. Replace any vague adjectives like 'innovative' or 'reliable' with the concrete behavior that makes them true. Flag any pillar that could apply to a different tool or professional without changing a word — those need to be rewritten. Second, for the competitive matrix, here is the brandy-style prompt: I am analyzing the competitive landscape for [personal brand / my Madison tool]. The three to five main comparators are: [list them by name]. The dimensions I want to compare across are: [list four to six — for example: technical depth, transparency, domain focus, output format, human oversight]. For each comparator, score each dimension high, medium, or low, and write one sentence explaining the score. Then identify the white space — the combination of dimensions where no comparator scores strongly — and write one sentence claiming that position.

props.segment: Positioning & Pillars: What Sets You Apart
props.command: Pillars: UVP [paste] → 5 pillars, specific, no vague adjectives. Matrix: comparators [list] · dimensions [list 4–6] → score each (H/M/L) + sentence + white space sentence.
props.runningText: flag any pillar that could apply to a different tool without changing a word — those need rewriting…

beats.narration_text: Positioning & Pillars: What Sets You Apart. Liam, in for Bear.
